assistant_output_excerpt:
I’m setting up the Rails app in the current workspace and first verifying what’s already here plus which Ruby/Rails versions `mise` exposes, so I can generate the project with the newest available toolchain and the minimal frameworks you asked for.
Plan: 1. Install the newest stable Ruby exposed by `mise`, then generate a Rails app in this directory with the minimal frameworks you asked for. 2. Add the chat UI, RubyLLM/OpenRouter integration, tests, and project docs/runtime files. 3. Wire up lint/security/test tooling and a basic CI workflow, then run a focused verification pass.
Ruby `4.0.2` is already present, so I’m activating it locally and then checking the newest Rails gem version available under that Ruby before generating the app skeleton.
I’ve confirmed the newest Rails gem version available here is `8.1.3`. Next I’m installing Rails, generating the app in-place with only the frameworks we need, and then I’ll layer in the RubyLLM/OpenRouter chat pieces on top of that skeleton.
I have the generator options now. I’m keeping Rails’ built-in RuboCop/Brakeman/bundle-audit/CI scaffolding, skipping the database and mail/job stacks, and generating the app with Tailwind plus Hotwire so the later chat UI stays idiomatic instead of becoming a single-file prototype.
The skeleton is in place. I’m generating the Rails-side chat/controller scaffolding next so the app structure stays generator-backed, then I’ll replace the defaults with the PORO-based conversation flow and the Turbo/Stimulus interface.
I’m on the dependency pass now: removing generator leftovers we don’t need, adding `ruby_llm` and `simplecov`, then I’ll switch into the application code so the controller/services/views line up with the no-database design.
The Rails generator left `package.json` incomplete here, so I’m fixing the frontend dependency state explicitly now. After that I’ll write the Rails files in one pass so the UI, services, and tests stay consistent.
The Node shim wasn’t active in this project yet, so I’m pinning the already-available local Node version in `mise` and then re-running the package install. That keeps the JS/Tailwind toolchain reproducible alongside the Ruby version.
I have enough context to do the main code pass. I’m replacing the generated stubs with a session-backed conversation store, a RubyLLM/OpenRouter service layer, modular Turbo/Stimulus views, and the supporting tests/config so the app is coherent end-to-end instead of a patched scaffold.
I’m on the last major edit pass now: tightening the Gemfile and project metadata, adding the tests and README, and replacing the generated container/CI defaults with versions that match this no-database app instead of the stock template.
I’m making two small correctness tweaks before verification: passing plain hashes into the form object and making `bin/setup` fail fast on `npm install`. Then I’ll run the formatter/lint/tests/build pass.
The app and asset builds are compiling. I’m in the verification pass now, starting with the Rails test suite so I can fix any structural issues before running lint and security checks.
Rails boot failed early on one config detail: `64.megabytes` is not available at that point in `config/application.rb`. I’m switching that to a plain byte count and rerunning the test suite immediately.
The first test run exposed three clean issues: one relative partial lookup in a view test, one chat-session reset expectation, and missing `Minitest` stubbing support in the test helper. I’m fixing those directly and rerunning the suite.
The remaining blocker is just the test double strategy: this Ruby/Minitest setup doesn’t expose `Object#stub`, so I’m swapping the controller tests over to an explicit temporary override of `ChatCompletion.new` and then rerunning the suite.
The override patch itself was fine; the shell heredoc just collided with the embedded Ruby heredoc name. I’m rerunning that edit with safe delimiters and continuing.
The last test failures are both in test cod

model.label: GPT 5.4 xHigh (Codex)
model.selection_reason: GPT 5.4 via Codex CLI (codex exec) at xhigh reasoning effort. Bypasses OpenRouter — uses OpenAI API directly. Required because GPT 5.4 does not support tool calling through OpenRouter, causing opencode to stall.
